title: Enhanced hyperspherical cosmology and TZP handoff
author: Daniel Alexander Trawin

sections.heading: Hubble constant as a breathing rate
sections.description: Defines the Hubble constant in a hyperspherical universe as the present-day fractional breathing frequency of the cosmic three-sphere, H0=\dot{R}_0/R_0, replacing the idea of galaxies moving through static space with a radius that expands.

sections.heading: Photon speed and cosmological geometry
sections.description: Explains that light locally always travels at c, but in curved expanding spacetime the coordinate distance depends on the scale factor. In hyperspherical geometry radial null geodesics satisfy d\chi/dt=c/R(t), so the angular speed diminishes as the radius grows, and redshift reflects cumulative stretching.

sections.heading: Redshift as a measure of cosmic breathing
sections.description: Shows that cosmological redshift measures the ratio of the hypersphere’s radius now to that at emission: 1+z=R0/Re, and expresses distances (angular diameter, luminosity) in terms of the sine of the comoving angle, linking redshift to the breathing history.

sections.heading: Inward and outward breathing states
sections.description: Classifies cosmic dynamics into four states based on the sign of the Hubble parameter H=\dot{R}/R and the second derivative \ddot{R}: outward acceleration, outward deceleration, inward acceleration and inward deceleration. Discusses how matter, radiation and dark energy influence these states via the Friedmann and acceleration equations.

sections.heading: Cosmic curvature and dynamic dark energy
sections.description: Discusses positive curvature (k=+1) in a closed hyperspherical universe and the smallness of the observed curvature parameter \Omega_K. Introduces the curvature radius Rc=c/(H0√|\Omega_K|) and notes that dynamic dark-energy models with evolving equation-of-state w(a) permit slight negative \Omega_K while fitting data.

sections.heading: Redefining the Big Bang
sections.description: Argues that the Big Bang need not be an absolute singularity; it may instead be a hot, acoustic phase transition within a pre-existing hyperspherical enclosure. Suggests quantum gravity or extra dimensions provide a finite minimum radius, and relates this to hot Big Bang evidence (CMB, BAO) without requiring a pointlike origin.

sections.heading: A dynamical breathing pressure
sections.description: Posits that dark energy represents a dynamic pressure driving the universe’s breathing rather than a fixed cosmological constant. Suggests an evolving w(a) could reconcile observations and treats breathing pressure as part of the generalised stress-energy content.

sections.heading: Bessel–curvature lock and dark-sector ratios
sections.description: Connects hyperspherical curvature with Bessel zero positions, defining \delta_4=1-\pi/j_{1,1}≈0.1801 and relating it to \Omega_K via √|\Omega_K| ≈ (3/8)\delta_4. Notes that combining the ratio 32/27 with 3/8 yields 4/9, mirroring the inferred ratio between topological dark matter and breathing pressure densities.

sections.heading: Dark energy as a breathing-volume phase; dark matter as a topological current
sections.description: Introduces a three-phase occupancy model: \phi_E for dark-energy (breathing-volume) phase, \phi_T for THZ/topological-current (dark-matter) phase, and \phi_b for baryonic matter. Dark energy is volumetric pressure, dark matter is a collisionless phase of topological currents, and visible matter condenses at a finite-viscosity interface where \eta_T= P_T/(f_ref) det(J_p).

sections.heading: Volumetric buoyancy and stillness
sections.description: Defines stillness as global volumetric force balance: the integral of force densities over a closed hypersphere vanishes. Uses the Elsasser number \Lambda=B^2/(\mu_0\rho\eta\Omega)≈1 to characterise neutral buoyancy where magnetic, rotational and resistive energies balance, providing stability at the interface.

sections.heading: Generalised hyperspherical enclosures
sections.description: Extends volumetric equilibrium to higher-dimensional spheres: S^3 has volume 2π^2R^3, S^4 has 8π^2R^4/3, and S^5 has π^3R^5. Notes breathing rates scale as nH and that the volume coefficients between S^3 and S^4 (4/3) and S^4 and S^5 (~1.1781) echo the ratio 32/27. Bessel zero positions shift accordingly, producing drops δ_4≈18.01% and δ_5≈30.08%.

sections.heading: Flip and twist seam dynamics
sections.description: Describes a topological seam implemented by a Gaussian window and a sine wave at the bridge frequency f_bridge=(32/27)f'. The seam includes a π phase flip and a linear phase ramp, producing a local winding Q_W and preserving global equilibrium. Provides an operational formula for the seam signal and its analytic-phase winding.

sections.heading: Dipole amplitude and the recycle horizon
sections.description: Defines \Xi_{RH}(z)=(1+z)\sqrt{|\Omega_K|}. The recycle horizon occurs when \Xi_{RH}=1, at z≈13.8 for \Omega_K≈-0.00456. Proposes testing whether the residual matter dipole grows as \Xi_{RH}→1 after removing kinematic and boundary contributions, noting current quasar samples probe only \Xi_{RH}≤0.2.

sections.heading: Cosmic filaments, BAO, and spin alignments
sections.description: Explains that BAO generate spherical interference shells (~150 Mpc) seeding filaments; quasar polarisations and galaxy spins align with filaments, indicating angular-momentum transfer. Observes a rotating filament at z≈0.032 with HI galaxy spins aligning (|cos ψ|≈0.64), supporting filamentary current flow.

sections.heading: Nested shells and local recycle systems
sections.description: Proposes that each scale—from cosmic hypersphere to BAO shells to filaments, galaxy groups, and black-hole horizons—acts as a recycle shell with its own breathing, rotation and flux pinning. Smaller shells oscillate faster (ω∝R^{-1}) and have higher energy density (ρ∝R^{-4}), linking local dynamics to the global breathing–interface paradigm.

sections.heading: d'Alembertian square as vector pyramid
sections.description: Recasts the box operator as a pyramid: the temporal second derivative is the apex and the spatial Laplacian components form the base. From above, the apex projects to the square center; the operator becomes a Minkowski-sign contraction of time-apex against spatial-base vectors.

sections.heading: Golden-ratio phase winding on the pyramid base
sections.description: Adds a fractional phase winding exp(i varphi theta) around the pyramid base. Because non-integer winding is multivalued on one sheet, it is interpreted as a branch-cut or multi-sheet projection rather than a closed single-valued vortex charge.

sections.heading: Delta-Gaussian impulse and toroidal coupling
sections.description: Uses a narrow Gaussian as a physical soft-Dirac delta to inject wideband spectral content. Its overlap with a toroidal Gaussian r^ell exp(-r^2/(2 sigma^2)) exp(i ell theta) is suppressed at the vortex core unless breathing, offset coupling or symmetry breaking gives finite support.

sections.heading: Two-particle zero-point branch and coherent excitation
sections.description: Places one system in a non-vacuum/toroidal state and the second in a zero-point field. A local interaction g delta(x1-x2) can produce entanglement, energy transfer and Lindblad-type decoherence, while preserving causality and global conservation.

sections.heading: Rhythmic classical drive and coupling gate
sections.description: Models brain-wave or other biological rhythms as classical band-limited drives s(t) that modulate toroidal width sigma(t), phase or coupling strength. The result is entrained overlap bursts and sidebands, not proof of consciousness-energy transfer.

sections.heading: Reverse harmonic dimensional decomposition
sections.description: Adds the adjoint inverse problem to forward harmonic expansion: physical configurations map to spectra, while reverse harmonic reconstruction maps observed frequencies back to quantum numbers, topology and candidate driver fields.

sections.heading: Thermodynamic consistency and Landauer bound
sections.description: Frames vacuum-gradient harvesting as a Casimir-like boundary-work protocol requiring full accounting of erasure, dissipation and reset costs. Energy extraction must be a free-energy difference, not a free-energy-from-nothing claim.

sections.heading: Curry-Howard-Lambek proof layer
sections.description: Organizes propositions as types, constructions as programs and physical mappings as categorical morphisms. The layer is useful for internal consistency and handoff, but empirical claims still require measurement.
